text.title: websocket-design Failure Modes
text.summary: Migrated antipatterns for websocket-design as failure modes. Add detector + repair clauses where missing.

failure-mode.description: No heartbeat: connections sit idle and the server never detects stale connections.
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: Stale connections accumulate and consume file descriptors. On Linux, the default `nofile` limit is 1024. 10k idle connections exhaust the limit and block new connections.

failure-mode.description: No per-connection or per-user limit on concurrent connections.
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: A single user or bot can open thousands of connections, exhausting server resources and starving legitimate users.

failure-mode.description: No authentication on the upgrade handshake — anonymous connections accepted, authorization checked after the first message.
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: Unauthenticated connections consume resources before rejection. If auth is checked after the first message, an attacker can open thousands of connections without sending any data.

failure-mode.description: Blocking or CPU-intensive operations inside async message handlers.
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: Async WebSocket handlers run on the event loop. A blocking call (file read, CPU hash, synchronous DB) stalls all other WebSocket connections on the same worker until it completes.

failure-mode.description: Sending large payloads (tens of KB or more) directly in WebSocket messages.
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: Large messages block the send buffer, introduce latency for other subscribers, and can cause memory pressure. Use HTTP for large payloads; send only a reference ID over WebSocket.

failure-mode.description: Subscription state keyed by socket object rather than user ID, causing authorization to leak across reconnects.
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: On reconnect, a new socket is created. If subscriptions are keyed by socket, old subscriptions linger. If keyed by user ID without revoking old sockets, a user might receive messages intended for a different session.
